title: 1 Kings 11
subtitle: Solomon's Apostasy: Foreign Wives, Adversaries, and the Kingdom Torn

sections.header: Verses 1–25 — Seven Hundred Wives Turn Solomon's Heart; God Raises Adversaries

heb.paragraph: Solomon loved manynāšîm nokriyyôt(v.1) — the exact category Deuteronomy forbids for kings (Deut 17:17) and that Proverbs repeatedly warns against (Prov 2:16; 5:20; 7:5). The irony is devastating: the author of Proverbs falls victim to the very sin he warned his son about.

"His wives turned his heart after other gods" (v.4) — the verbnāṭāh(to turn, incline, bend) describes a gradual process, not a sudden collapse. Solomon's apostasy was not a single dramatic act but a slow erosion of devotion over decades.

panels.ctx: Chapter 11 is the most devastating chapter in 1 Kings. The wisest man who ever lived, who built God's temple, who prayed the greatest prayer in the OT — turns to other gods. Seven hundred wives and three hundred concubines from forbidden nations turn his heart to Ashtoreth (Sidonian), Chemosh (Moabite), and Molek (Ammonite). The narrator uses the word "heart" five times — the same heart that asked for wisdom at Gibeon (3:9) now follows other gods. God responds by raising three adversaries: Hadad the Edomite, Rezon of Damascus, and Jeroboam son of Nebat. The divine discipline is precise: the kingdom that Solomon's wisdom unified will be torn apart by the adversaries his sin provoked.

cross.note: "He must not take many wives, or his heart will be led astray." The Deuteronomic warning is fulfilled word for word.
"Was it not because of marriages like these that Solomon king of Israel sinned?" Nehemiah cites Solomon as the canonical example of foreign-wife apostasy.
"For the lips of the adulterous woman drip honey... but in the end she is bitter as gall." Solomon's own wisdom, tragically unheeded by its author.

notes.note: Calvin: Solomon's fall teaches that no degree of past blessing guarantees future faithfulness. Wisdom received is not wisdom retained. The greatest gifts require the greatest vigilance, for the higher the position, the more catastrophic the fall.

Calvin: Building shrines to Molek — the god of child sacrifice — on the hill facing the temple is an act of breathtaking spiritual violence. The man who built God's house now builds houses for his enemies. Sin does not merely subtract from devotion; it actively constructs alternatives.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note:min-haggôyim ʾăšer ʾāmar-YHWH(from the nations about which the LORD said) — the narrator explicitly cites the Deuteronomic prohibition. This is not ambiguous: Solomon's marriages violate a specific, known divine command.

NET Note:lĕbābô lōʾ-hāyāh šālēm ʿim-YHWH(his heart was not fully with the LORD) —šālēm(complete, whole, at peace) is the adjective from the same root asšālômandŠĕlōmōh(Solomon). The man whose name means "peace/wholeness" is no longer whole with God. The irony is built into his name.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Wiseman: Diplomatic marriages were standard ANE practice for sealing alliances. Egyptian, Hittite, and Mesopotamian kings maintained large harems for political reasons. But Solomon's 700 wives far exceeds documented royal harems. Ashurnasirpal II had hundreds but not thousands. The number signals excess even by ANE standards.

Wiseman: Chemosh was the national deity of Moab (attested on the Mesha Stele, c.840 BC). Molek (Milcom) was an Ammonite deity associated with fire rituals. The high places on the "hill east of Jerusalem" (the Mount of Olives) would have been visible from the temple itself — a deliberate affront.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Provan: The narrator constructs ch. 11 as the mirror-image of ch. 3. In ch. 3, Solomon loved God "except" for the high places. In ch. 11, his heart is no longer fully devoted — the "except" has consumed the whole. The flaw that was tolerable in youth has become fatal in old age.

Provan: "His heart was not fully devoted to the LORD his God, as the heart of David his father had been." The comparison with David is the Deuteronomistic standard. David sinned grievously but always returned. Solomon drifted and never returned. The difference is not in the magnitude of sin but in the direction of the heart.

sections.header: Verses 26–43 — Ahijah Tears the Cloak: Ten Tribes for Jeroboam; Solomon Dies

heb.paragraph: Ahijah tears the new cloak into twelve pieces and gives Jeroboam ten (vv.30–31) — anôt(enacted prophecy / sign-act). The physical tearing symbolises what God will do to the kingdom. Tearing a garment was a recognised prophetic action (cf. 1 Sam 15:27–28). The prophecy is both prediction and divine sentence.

God preserves one tribe for David's sake — "so that David my servant may always have anērbefore me in Jerusalem" (v.36). The lamp is the Davidic dynasty, kept burning even in judgment. The covenant with David is not revoked but reduced: the dynasty continues, but over one tribe instead of twelve.

panels.ctx: Ahijah's prophecy to Jeroboam is one of the most dramatic enacted prophecies in the OT. He meets Jeroboam alone in a field, tears his new cloak into twelve pieces, and gives ten to Jeroboam — ten tribes will be his. But one tribe remains with David's house "for the sake of David my servant and for the sake of Jerusalem." The prophecy is both judgment (the kingdom torn) and grace (the lamp preserved). Solomon tries to kill Jeroboam, who flees to Egypt until Solomon's death. The chapter closes with the standard formula: Solomon reigned 40 years. The wisest king dies under God's discipline, and his kingdom is about to shatter.

cross.note: Saul's robe torn by Samuel — the first enacted tearing prophecy. "The LORD has torn the kingdom of Israel from you today." Ahijah's action deliberately echoes Samuel's.
"When he does wrong, I will punish him... But my love will never be taken away from him." The Davidic covenant promises discipline but not abandonment. One tribe preserved is the covenant kept.

"This turn of events was from the LORD, to fulfil the word the LORD had spoken to Jeroboam through Ahijah." The narrator confirms that the political catastrophe of ch. 12 executes the prophetic word of ch. 11.

notes.note: Calvin: The tearing of the cloak is a sermon in fabric. God's judgments are not hidden but demonstrated — made visible, tangible, public. Let no one imagine that divine discipline operates only in the invisible realm. God's hand in history can be seen by those with eyes to see.

Calvin: The preserved lamp teaches that God's mercy outlasts human sin. Solomon deserved the complete loss of his kingdom. Instead, one tribe is preserved — not for Solomon's sake but for David's, and ultimately for the sake of the Messiah who would come from David's line. Grace plans ahead.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: The garment (śalmāh ḥădāšāh) is specifically "new" — unused, whole, representing the intact kingdom. Its tearing into twelve pieces is a powerful visual: what was whole and new is now fragmented. The Hebrewqāraʿdenotes violent tearing, not cutting.

NET Note:nēr(lamp) functions as a metaphor for dynastic continuity — the lamp kept burning in the temple (Exod 27:20) symbolises the perpetual presence of the Davidic house before God. When the lamp metaphor is used for Judah's kings, it means the dynasty survives.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Wiseman: Jeroboam's flight to Shishak (Shoshenq I) in Egypt places this event in the last decade of Solomon's reign (c.940–930 BC). Shoshenq I founded Egypt's 22nd Dynasty and would later invade Judah (14:25–26). His willingness to harbour Jeroboam suggests Egyptian interest in destabilising the Israelite monarchy.

Wiseman: "The book of the annals of Solomon" is one of three source documents cited in Kings (along with the annals of the kings of Israel and Judah). These were likely official court chronicles maintained by the royal scribes — thesōpērmentioned in Solomon's cabinet (4:3).

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Provan: The tearing of the cloak enacts what Solomon's sin has caused. The kingdom is not torn by Jeroboam's ambition but by Solomon's apostasy. The narrator places the cause (11:1–13) before the effect (11:26–40) to make the theological logic unmistakable: sin causes division.

Provan: The lamp metaphor is the Deuteronomistic historian's way of expressing eschatological hope within a narrative of decline. The kingdom falls, the kings fail, but the lamp is never extinguished. The entire narrative of Kings is held together by this fragile, persistent flame.

ppl.name: Solomon
ppl.text: The divine discipline is precise: the kingdom that Solomon's wisdom unified will be torn apart by the adversaries his sin provoked…

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: But one tribe remains with David's house "for the sake of David my servant and for the sake of Jerusalem…

ppl.name: Jeroboam
ppl.text: God responds by raising three adversaries: Hadad the Edomite, Rezon of Damascus, and Jeroboam son of Nebat…

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: The wisest man who ever lived, who built God's temple, who prayed the greatest prayer in the OT — turns to other gods…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThey were from nations about which the Lord had told the Israelites, \u201cYou must not intermarry with them, because they will surely turn your hearts after their gods.\u201d Nevertheless, Solomon held fast to them in love.ESVfrom the nations concerning which the LORD had said to the people of Israel, \u201cYou shall not enter into marriage with them, neither shall they with you, for surely they will turn away your heart after their gods.\u201d Solomon clung to these in love.NIVHe followed Ashtoreth the goddess of the Sidonians, and Molek the detestable god of the Ammonites.ESVFor Solomon went after Ashtoreth the goddess of the Sidonians, and after Milcom the abomination of the Ammonites.
